description: Calendula is a cheerful, hardy annual with vibrant orange or yellow blooms. It's incredibly easy to grow and highly beneficial in the garden, attracting pollinators and acting as a 'trap crop' for pests. The petals are edible and have many traditional medicinal uses.

september: 🌱 Sow seeds outdoors (for early spring flowers)
november: 🍂 Clear dead plants or let self-seed

care.sowIndoors: Sow in modules in March or April. Cover with 1cm of compost. Keep at 15–20°C. Seeds sprout in 7–14 days.
care.sowOutdoors: Sow directly into flowering position from April to May, or in September for earlier blooms next year. Sow 1cm deep in drills 20cm apart.
care.hardenOff: Gradually acclimatise indoor-sown seedlings to the outdoors over a week in May before planting out.
care.plantOut: Space plants 20–30cm apart in a sunny spot. They are unfussy about soil but prefer good drainage.
care.watering: Water regularly when first planted and during dry summer spells. Avoid wetting the foliage to prevent mildew.
care.feeding: Generally doesn't need much feed. A light balanced fertiliser in early summer is sufficient.
care.harvest: Pick flower heads regularly to encourage continuous blooming. Petals can be dried for tea or used fresh in salads.
care.overwintering: Hardy annual. Will survive light frosts. Pull up at the end of the season or let it self-seed for next year.

propagation.seedDescription: Curved, bumpy, C-shaped seeds that look like dried caterpillars. Easy to handle and sow.

deadheading.notes: Essential to keep the plant blooming. Remove the entire flower stalk once the bloom has faded.

seedSaving.method: Allow a few flower heads to dry on the plant. The seeds will turn brown and detach easily when ready. Store in a paper envelope.
seedSaving.seedPodDescription: The center of the flower dries into a cluster of curved, brown seeds.

commonProblems: Powdery Mildew — white coating on leaves in late summer. Improve spacing and water at base.
Aphids — common on stems. Can be hosed off or left as a trap crop for beneficial insects.
Slugs and Snails — protect young seedlings in spring.
funFact: Known as 'Poor Man's Saffron' because its petals can be used to add a golden yellow color to rice, butter, and cheese!
